title: Defining the Problem: What Are We Predicting?
concept: Churn prediction asks a machine learning model to output a binary label — will this customer cancel or not — using raw signals like watch hours, plan type, and support activity as its input.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a feedback loop in machine learning?

options.text: When a model's predictions influence the future data it's later trained on, creating a self-reinforcing cycle.
A method for saving a trained model to disk.
A technique for splitting data into train and test sets.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Feedback loops happen when predictions shape the very data used to retrain the model later.

cards.title: The quiet goodbye
cards.body: Every month, some of StreamBox's subscribers just... stop. No complaint, no phone call — their card gets charged, they don't watch anything, and next month they cancel. By the time StreamBox notices, the customer is already gone.

cards.title: Churn prediction = binary classification
cards.body: Churn means a customer cancels their subscription. Predicting churn is a binary classification problem: for each customer, the model outputs one of two labels — churned: yes or churned: no.

cards.title: A checkup before the symptoms show
cards.body: Predicting churn is like a doctor checking vital signs before a patient feels sick. StreamBox isn't waiting for the cancellation email — it's reading the early signs (falling watch time, a failed payment) to flag risk in advance.

cards.title: What StreamBox already has
cards.body: StreamBox's database holds real signals for all 500,000 subscribers: watch_hours (last 30 days), plan_type (Basic/Standard/Premium), support_tickets (count filed), signup_date, and payment_failures (recent declined charges). None of these say "churn" directly — the model has to learn the connection.

cards.title: Not fortune-telling
cards.body: You might think a churn model predicts exactly why a specific person will cancel. In reality, it only estimates a probability — like "this customer has a 78% chance of canceling next month" — based on patterns across thousands of past customers, not a certainty about any one person.

cards.title: Target vs. Raw Data
cards.body: What the model predicts vs. what it's given to work with:

exercises.prompt: What is the target variable StreamBox wants to predict?
exercises.explain: Correct! churned: yes/no is exactly what the model is trying to predict — everything else is an input.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Predicting whether a customer will churn is a regression problem.
exercises.explain: False! Churn has only two possible outcomes (yes/no), which makes it a classification problem, not regression.

exercises.sentence: Because churn only has two possible outcomes, it's framed as a ___ classification problem.
exercises.explain: Binary classification means the model chooses between exactly two labels: churned or not churned.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a piece of raw input data, not the target variable?
exercises.explain: Correct! support_tickets is one of the raw signals fed into the model — churned is the label it's trying to predict.

exercises.prompt: StreamBox's data team pulls together watch_hours, plan_type, support_tickets, signup_date, and payment_failures for 500,000 customers, plus a record of who actually canceled last month. How should this be framed as a machine learning problem?

options.text: Binary classification: use the five signals as input features to predict a churned: yes/no label for each customer.
Regression: use churned status to predict the exact number of watch hours next month.
Binary classification: use signup_date alone, ignoring every other column.
This can't be modeled without knowing exactly why each customer canceled.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Five raw signals become the input features, and the model learns to output a binary churned: yes/no label. Next: before any of that data can train a model, StreamBox has to clean it up first!
